title: What was being an Eva fan like before the CI
display_title: What was being an Eva fan like before the CI
wikitext:
[]: What was being an Eva fan like before the CI? Post by Th3Marauder » Tue Jul 02, 2013 3:00 pm I've only been an Evangelion fan for about 2 years now, and for someone that tries not to get sucked into "fandoms" its become a huge part of my life. And not like someone in the "Doctor Who Fandom" or the "Sherlock Fandom" just because they really like the show. Eva is a big part of my life because it's just the best that anime, television and pretty much media has ever been. I'm also lucky enough to have come into the fanbase well after the release of NGE & EoE, and a few years after 1.11 and 2.22 came out, so I had a lot to go on, plus every online knew exactly what they were talking about, which really helped me get into Eva. But it mustn't have always been like that. And ObsessiveMathsFreak comment on the 14 Year Mystery thread got me wondering, just what was it like being an Eva fan, especially on these forums, before we had the CI from NGE2 and Blu-Ray and all the stuff that helps us decode what the hell is going on in this crazy franchise?

[]: Post by NemZ » Tue Jul 02, 2013 3:24 pm Basically me, but less irritated.

[]: Post by Chuckman » Tue Jul 02, 2013 3:25 pm People had to think for themselves, it must have been terrifying.

[]: Post by Ornette » Tue Jul 02, 2013 3:55 pm Go through the http://forum.evageeks.org/forum/archive/ archive, especially anything older than 2007-2008.

[]: Post by Kendrix » Tue Jul 02, 2013 3:57 pm Well, when you look at old fanfics or fics from non-english communitiers that may not have all the translations, it wasn't that different. Interpretations/original content featuring the angels and adam/Lillith tended to be more fantasy and less Sci-fi/some variation of their mythological namesakes, but for the most part, ppl would argue about character interactions etc as we do now, lots of stuff such backstory details as the FAR (whose existence is technically deductable from the "identical artificial sperical cavities" line in ep 21.) technically has little bearing on the mainnarrative; Misato and Shinji were only scraping away at the edges of all that was going on. It's kinda sad that the early days stuff did a lot more with Kaji; It's like ppl consider him "solved" ever since we learned that he was killed by a SEELE goon...

[]: Post by Chuckman » Tue Jul 02, 2013 4:04 pm We were told that he was killed by a Seele goon. Why do people want to be told things? He was killed by his choice to put his quest for the truth ahead of his love for another person, a casualty of obsession. That's more important than who pulled the trigger. If who pulled the trigger was important, the show itself would give us enough information to discern who it was, as it does with all the important points. Besides, it's glaringly obvious the triggerman was Pen Pen, who is the archangel Gabriel in avian form.

[]: Post by Xard » Tue Jul 02, 2013 4:12 pm The one thing that I remember from dark ages in really traumatic sense would be the old Asuka/Misato/Rei hybrid theory about One More Final brrrr

[]: Post by bobbyfischer's ghost » Tue Jul 02, 2013 4:16 pm @Xard Let's not forget the pregnant Asuka theory. ugh Chuckman wrote: He was killed by his choice to put his quest for the truth ahead of his love for another person, a casualty of obsession. That's more important than who pulled the trigger. If who pulled the trigger was important, the show itself would give us enough information to discern who it was, as it does with all the important points.

[]: Post by Chuckman » Tue Jul 02, 2013 4:19 pm Xard wrote: The one thing that I remember from dark ages in really traumatic sense would be the old Asuka/Misato/Rei hybrid theory about One More Final brrrr

[]: Post by Ornette » Tue Jul 02, 2013 4:19 pm If you look at something like Eva-R which was written to be a serious continuation of Evangelion, but written in 1998, when there was almost nothing like CRC's, interviews, production notes, storyboards, etc. And what very little was released back then, even smaller amount (and usually only excerpts) were translated. A lot of the work that went into analyzing the details of the show in the English language happened on the Evangelion Mailing List. You can still go through the EML at http://eva.onegeek.org/ to read through some discussion on how little people knew about the details of the fictional world. And if you read through something like Eva-R, which used the analysis from back then. you'll see a lot of inconsistencies with what was later put together with the help of actual interviews, production notes, story boards, etc. People often slammed Eva-R because of how wrong things in the Eva world were interpreted but it's really not the guy's fault. It's a good indication of what little people figured out back then. Chuckman wrote: We were told that he was killed by a Seele goon. Why do people want to be told things?

[]: Post by Chuckman » Tue Jul 02, 2013 4:30 pm Ornette wrote: People were told by Anno that it wasn't Misato that killed him. The entire fandom, both Japanese and international, thought it was Misato. Later, the cut was retconned so the gunshot didn't immediately follow the nameplate on Misato's apartment. Sure, anyone with an IQ of over 100 figured that bit out, after that, the next question one would ask if they wanted to dig deeper, after the "why" was spoonfed to us, is the "who".

[]: Post by Stillborn » Tue Jul 02, 2013 4:37 pm For many people, work suggests Misato killed Kaji. It was revealed to be untrue. Without that reveal, many people would still cling to wrong idea.

[]: Post by Ornette » Tue Jul 02, 2013 4:42 pm Well, the who shot Kaji is kind of a useless piece of pedantry, but there's a lot of other reveals that create a solid basis of interpretation, like when Sadamoto talked in an interview about seeing a documentary about the A-10 Nerve and bringing the mother-child linkage as a major theme in how piloting Evas work. Before that, there was a tenuous connection. People thought you could ride evas like horses and that there wasn't any intimate connection aside from Eva-01 and some edge cases in the rest of the show. Knowing that there must be a mother-child connection in order to synch, does that add more to the enjoyment of the show? Maybe not for some people, but it provides a more solid basis for interpreting other events in the show, both technically and thematically.

[]: Post by Chuckman » Tue Jul 02, 2013 4:42 pm Why is it "wrong"? The text apparently supports it for those who read it that way. It is then not "wrong", only different. Anno's reading is one reading, not the only reading. He may intend for it to be read one way, but creators often insert meanings into their work that they don't intend, or intend certain meanings and fail to bring them across. There is no truth or falsehood here, it's a work of fiction. It's all lies, but they're entertaining lies, and in the end, isn't that the real truth? @Ornette: It's never stated in exactly those terms in the show, but all of that information is there in one form or another. The Evas are haunted, Shinji's mother is in the Eva, Asuka confirms the same of hers, Rei says the pilot has to open their heart, etc. The need to synchronize with the Eva, that they're not just a machine you turn on, is a plot point in multiple places in the narrative. There's plenty of thematic meat there without any of it being spelled out.

[]: Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Jul 02, 2013 4:52 pm Xard wrote: The one thing that I remember from dark ages in really traumatic sense would be the old Asuka/Misato/Rei hybrid theory about One More Final brrrr

[]: Post by AlphaGamma » Tue Jul 02, 2013 4:57 pm Chuckman wrote: Why is it "wrong"? The text apparently supports it for those who read it that way. It is then not "wrong", only different. Anno's reading is one reading, not the only reading. He may intend for it to be read one way, but creators often insert meanings into their work that they don't intend, or intend certain meanings and fail to bring them across.

[]: Post by Ornette » Tue Jul 02, 2013 4:59 pm Chuckman wrote: @Ornette: It's never stated in exactly those terms in the show, but all of that information is there in one form or another. The Evas are haunted, Shinji's mother is in the Eva, Asuka confirms the same of hers, Rei says the pilot has to open their heart, etc. The need to synchronize with the Eva, that they're not just a machine you turn on, is a plot point in multiple places in the narrative. There's plenty of thematic meat there without any of it being spelled out.

[]: Post by Xard » Tue Jul 02, 2013 5:04 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: The funny thing about that theory is that the movie explicitly told the viewer what was going on with Yui's dialogue with Fuyutsuki, thus proving it to be a false theory. No CI was needed at all to decode what was going on there. It was almost as if people were going out of their way to be that confused about that scene.

[]: Post by Chuckman » Tue Jul 02, 2013 6:11 pm Ornette wrote: Sure, and that's why there were people who thought you could aribtrarily pilot evas outside of 01 and 02, there's plenty of evidence that could be manufactured to support such a claim. And, while trying to keep to the topic of what the fandom was like before extratextual stuff was readily available, there were dozens of competing interpretations of how Evas worked, all of them had plenty of evidence. It's sort of a problem when you tell a story as if you've planned out how the world works, but leave out all the details, everyone's going to have incompatible interpretations. Now, I'm not saying there's anything wrong with that, but, like if you look through pre-2003 EML posts or old ANF posts in the archive here, discussion was pretty much people butting heads, getting absolutely nowhere, since everyone is right and everyone is wrong. Some people are into that kind of stuff, other people just want to know if something is "right" or "wrong".

[]: Post by Stillborn » Tue Jul 02, 2013 6:20 pm Chuckman wrote: Also the FAR thing is dumb, the series makes much more sense if mankind is using technology to (poorly) grapple with literal magic, and that's how the show presents it.
